Here stands a vision of terrible beauty: the wrath of God about to be poured out in full, yet the chapter opens not with destruction but with the victorious singing of the redeemed. John sees those who conquered the beast standing on a sea of glass mixed with fire, harps in hand, voices raised in a song that echoes Moses and celebrates the Lamb. They sing of God's great and wonderful works, His just and true ways. This is the reality that frames everything to come: judgment belongs to a God whose righteousness is beyond question, whose holiness is absolute, whose rule over all nations is certain and eternal. The vision does not ask us to sympathize with those about to be judged or to question whether God's wrath is warranted. Instead it places us with the victors, singing their song, recognizing that those who stand firm against the beast are the ones who see clearly what God is truly like.

Then the temple opens. Seven angels—the final instruments of God's judgment—emerge dressed in spotless linen with golden sashes, clothed in purity and authority. One of the four living creatures, those beings who surround God's throne crying "Holy, holy, holy," gives them seven bowls filled with the wrath of God. The temple fills with smoke so thick that no one can enter it until the plagues are finished. This is not incidental detail. The presence of God—His glory and power made visible—completely fills the sanctuary. His judgment is not separate from His holiness; it flows from it. And the sealing of the temple declares that what is about to happen is God's final word, His completed work, a judgment that will not be interrupted or questioned or appealed.

What Revelation 15 demands of you is not comfort but clarity. The God who judges is the God who is holy, whose ways are just, whose works are wonderful. The same God whose glory filled the temple and whose wrath is about to be unleashed is the God you worship. If you have conquered through faith in the Lamb, you stand with those singing on the sea of glass, your voice part of an eternal chorus affirming that God's judgment is right. If you have not, this chapter speaks a warning that should move you to repentance—not to a God who delights in judgment, but to a God whose holiness permits nothing less. Either way, the message is inescapable: God's character is not negotiable, and His judgment is not delayed forever.
